name: Surrogate Primary Key (auto-increment / id)
definition: A synthetic, meaningless primary key (e.g. a serial/identity or UUID) that uniquely identifies each row instead of using business data.
pros: Stable — never changes even if business data does
No risk of leaking/colliding on real-world values
cons: Doesn't enforce business uniqueness on its own
Two rows can be identical except the id without a unique constraint
whenToUse: Almost every table — the default primary key for entities like users, orders, products.
whenNotToUse: Pure junction tables where the composite of two FKs is already a perfect natural key.

name: Natural Key
definition: Using real-world data that's inherently unique (e.g. email, ISBN, country code) as the primary key.
pros: Meaningful — the key itself carries information
cons: Business values change (email updates) and cascade everywhere
Often wide/multi-column → bloated FKs and indexes
whenToUse: Small stable reference tables (country codes, currency codes) where the value truly never changes.
whenNotToUse: Mutable or user-supplied values like email or username — use a surrogate key + a UNIQUE constraint instead.

name: Composite Primary Key
definition: A primary key made of two or more columns together, common on junction tables (e.g. (order_id, product_id)).
cons: Wider foreign keys if other tables reference it
whenToUse: Junction tables, or where a row is uniquely identified only by a combination of columns.
whenNotToUse: Core entity tables that other tables reference a lot — a surrogate id is cleaner there.

name: UUID vs Auto-increment Choice
definition: Deciding between a sequential integer id and a UUID for the surrogate key.
pros: UUIDs are unguessable and generatable client-side / across shards
Serials are compact, sortable, and index-friendly
cons: Random UUIDs hurt index locality and are larger
Serials leak row counts and don't work well across distributed inserts
whenToUse: UUID when ids are exposed publicly or generated in many places; serial/bigserial for internal, single-DB tables.
whenNotToUse: Don't default to random UUID PKs on huge write-heavy tables without considering index bloat (UUIDv7/ULID help).

name: Foreign Key Constraint
definition: A column that references another table's primary key, enforcing referential integrity (you can't point at a row that doesn't exist).
cons: Needs an index to keep joins/deletes fast
whenToUse: Every relationship between tables — this is the backbone of a relational schema.
whenNotToUse: Rarely omitted; only in extreme write-throughput cases where integrity is enforced elsewhere (a deliberate, documented tradeoff).

name: One-to-Many via Foreign Key
definition: Model 'one X has many Y' by putting the X id as a foreign key column on the Y table (e.g. order_id on order_items).
pros: Efficient lookups of children by parent
cons: Child can belong to exactly one parent
whenToUse: A customer has many orders; an order has many line items; a post has many comments.
whenNotToUse: When each side can relate to many of the other — that needs a junction table.

name: Junction / Associative Table (Many-to-Many)
definition: A table holding two foreign keys to resolve a many-to-many relationship into two one-to-many relationships (e.g. order_items, post_tags).
pros: The correct way to model many-to-many
Can carry attributes about the relationship
whenToUse: Students↔courses, orders↔products, posts↔tags, users↔roles — any many-to-many.
whenNotToUse: A simple one-to-many — adding a junction table there is over-engineering.

name: Self-Referencing FK (Hierarchy / Adjacency List)
definition: A foreign key on a table that points back to the same table, modeling trees like categories or org charts (parent_id).
cons: Recursive queries needed for deep traversal
Can't easily get full subtree in one simple query
whenToUse: Category trees, comment threads, employee→manager, nested folders.
whenNotToUse: Flat data with no hierarchy, or when you need heavy subtree queries (consider closure table/path enumeration).

name: Attributes on the Junction Table
definition: Storing data about the relationship itself on the junction row (e.g. quantity and unit_price on order_items, role granted_at).
pros: Captures relationship-specific facts in the right place
Avoids duplicating data on either parent
cons: Turns a 'pure' link table into an entity
whenToUse: order_items.quantity, enrollment.grade, membership.joined_at.
whenNotToUse: When the relationship truly has no attributes — keep the link table minimal.

name: Polymorphic Association
definition: One column pair (type + id) that points to rows in different tables (e.g. comment on either a post or a photo).
pros: Flexible — one comments table for many parents
cons: Cannot use real foreign keys → loses referential integrity
whenToUse: Rarely — only when many parent types share identical child structure and integrity can be tolerated.
whenNotToUse: Usually avoid in interviews: prefer separate FK columns or separate tables so real FKs work. A classic over-engineering trap.

name: Normalize to 3NF
definition: Organize tables so each fact lives in exactly one place — no repeating groups, every non-key column depends on the whole key and nothing but the key.
pros: No redundant data to drift out of sync
cons: Can be slower for read-heavy reporting
whenToUse: The default for transactional (OLTP) schemas — start normalized.
whenNotToUse: Don't over-normalize tiny value lists into ten tables; and analytics/reporting may deliberately denormalize.

name: Deliberate Denormalization
definition: Intentionally duplicating data (e.g. storing order_total, or a cached count) to avoid expensive joins/aggregations.
cons: Redundant data can drift out of sync
Premature denormalization is a common mistake
whenToUse: Proven read hotspots, reporting tables, or immutable snapshots (e.g. price captured on the order at purchase time).
whenNotToUse: Up front, before you have a measured read problem — that's classic over-engineering.

name: Lookup / Reference Table
definition: A small table of allowed values (statuses, categories, countries) that other tables reference by FK instead of free-text.
pros: One source of truth for allowed values
cons: Overkill for a tiny fixed set you could CHECK
whenToUse: Sets of values that change over time or carry their own attributes (categories, plan types).
whenNotToUse: A truly fixed 2–3 value set — a CHECK constraint or enum is simpler.

name: NOT NULL Constraint
definition: Requires a column to always have a value, encoding 'this fact is mandatory' into the schema.
cons: Needs a default or value on insert
whenToUse: Required fields: foreign keys, names, created_at, money amounts.
whenNotToUse: Genuinely optional attributes (middle_name, deleted_at) — NULL is meaningful there.

name: UNIQUE Constraint
definition: Guarantees no two rows share the same value(s) in a column or column set (e.g. UNIQUE(email), UNIQUE(order_id, product_id)).
pros: Enforces business uniqueness the PK doesn't
Backed by an index → fast lookups
Stops duplicate links on junction tables
whenToUse: Emails/usernames, and to prevent duplicate relationships on junction tables.
whenNotToUse: Columns that legitimately repeat (status, city).

name: CHECK Constraint
definition: Enforces a rule on column values (e.g. price >= 0, end_date > start_date, status IN (...)).
pros: Bad data can't enter the table
Encodes business rules in the schema
cons: Complex rules belong in app logic
whenToUse: Non-negative amounts, valid date ranges, small fixed status sets, percentage 0–100.
whenNotToUse: Multi-row or cross-table rules — those need triggers or app logic.

name: ON DELETE Behavior (CASCADE / RESTRICT / SET NULL)
definition: Defines what happens to child rows when a referenced parent is deleted.
pros: RESTRICT protects against accidental data loss
cons: CASCADE can delete far more than intended
Wrong choice causes orphans or surprise deletions
whenToUse: CASCADE for owned children (order → order_items); RESTRICT for references you must not orphan (don't delete a product still on orders).
whenNotToUse: Don't blanket-CASCADE everything — think per relationship.

name: Index on Foreign Key
definition: An index on each FK column so joins and 'find children of this parent' lookups (and cascading deletes) are fast.
cons: Most DBs do NOT auto-index FKs (only the referenced PK)
whenToUse: Virtually every foreign key column you join or filter on.
whenNotToUse: A tiny table scanned entirely anyway, or an FK you never query by.

name: Composite Index
definition: An index on multiple columns in order, serving queries that filter/sort by that column prefix (e.g. (tenant_id, created_at)).
pros: One index serves multi-column filters and sorts
cons: Doesn't help queries that skip the leading column
whenToUse: When you frequently filter by A and B together, or filter by A and sort by B.
whenNotToUse: When queries hit the columns independently — separate indexes may serve better.

name: Covering Index
definition: An index that includes all columns a query needs, so the DB answers from the index without touching the table.
pros: Avoids table lookups → very fast reads
whenToUse: A proven hot query that selects a few columns filtered by indexed ones.
whenNotToUse: Speculatively — adding covering indexes before measuring is over-engineering.

name: Partial / Filtered Index
definition: An index over only the rows matching a condition (e.g. WHERE deleted_at IS NULL, or status='active').
pros: Less write overhead than a full index
cons: Only helps queries matching the predicate
whenToUse: Indexing only active/non-deleted rows, or a unique constraint scoped to active rows.
whenNotToUse: When queries span all rows regardless of the condition.

name: Money as DECIMAL / Integer Cents
definition: Store currency as DECIMAL(n,2) or integer minor units (cents) — never floating point.
cons: Integer cents needs care on display
whenToUse: Any monetary amount: prices, totals, balances, invoice line items.
whenNotToUse: Never use FLOAT/DOUBLE for money — a guaranteed interview red flag.

name: Timestamps in UTC (timestamptz)
definition: Store timestamps with time zone / in UTC, converting to local time only at display.
cons: Naive timestamp columns lose zone info
whenToUse: created_at, updated_at, event times — basically all timestamps.
whenNotToUse: Pure calendar dates with no time/zone meaning (use DATE).

name: Soft Delete (deleted_at)
definition: Mark rows deleted with a flag/timestamp instead of removing them, filtering them out of normal queries.
cons: Every query must filter deleted rows
Unique constraints must be scoped (partial index)
whenToUse: User-facing records that may need recovery or audit (accounts, posts, orders).
whenNotToUse: High-churn ephemeral data, or when compliance requires real deletion.

name: Audit Columns (created_at / updated_at)
definition: Standard timestamp (and often created_by/updated_by) columns on every table to track when rows change.
pros: Enables debugging, sorting by recency, sync
cons: updated_at needs a trigger or app discipline
whenToUse: Essentially every entity table.
whenNotToUse: Immutable append-only event rows where created_at alone suffices.

name: History / Event Table over Mutable Status
definition: Instead of overwriting a single status column, append rows to a history/events table to keep the full timeline.
pros: Full audit trail of state changes
cons: More rows and a join to get current state
Often paired with a cached current_status
whenToUse: Order/shipment/subscription lifecycles where history matters.
whenNotToUse: Trivial flags where only the current value matters — a status column is enough.

name: Tenant ID Column (Multi-tenancy)
definition: Add tenant_id (org/account id) to tenant-owned tables and include it in keys, indexes, and every query to isolate customers.
cons: Must remember it in every query (risk of cross-tenant leak)
whenToUse: Shared-schema multi-tenant SaaS where each row belongs to one customer org.
whenNotToUse: Single-tenant apps, or when isolation requires separate databases/schemas.
